notes: searches: ~11 (10 shared, 1 straggler for GC-20-016 Wolff/Albury). WebFetch: 7 attempted, 3 informative (nonegw.org, essaysbyellenwhite.wordpress.com, LLU catalog stub); ESDA and Spectrum returned 403. Local pool used: Wellcome 1874 (primary insider account of Litch's calculation and the 1840 crisis, incl. Moniteur Ottoman of 22 Aug 1840 and the Rifat Bey quarantine detail); Bliss 1853 checked, no Ottoman content; Land AUSS 1994 is a scan and the environment lacks pdftoppm, so it could not be read (non-blocking: Sandeen and the primary record carry the rulings). No Phase-1 defense objects exist for any claim in this dossier (all three carried verifier 'disputed', which skipped the defender stage); the Phase-1 verifier rationales are answered inside each record. Direction check (charter 7.1): one ruling moved toward the book (GC-18-119 to well-supported), two moved against as written with high weakened rulings; both directions represented.
evidence_review: The dossier covers two evidence pools that meet in GC chapters 18 and 20. (1) The Litch/1840 pool. Josiah Litch's 'The Probability of the Second Coming of Christ, about A.D. 1843' (June 1838) applied the year-day reading of Revelation 9 to Ottoman history: 150 years from Othman's attack of 27 July 1299 to 1449 (Constantine 'Deacozes' ascending 'by permission' of the Sultan), then 391 years 15 days ending in August 1840 (Wellcome 1874, lines 3841-3862, 6996-7069, reproduces the whole scheme from Litch's own text). In Signs of the Times, and Expositor of Prophecy of 1 Aug 1840 Litch pinned the terminus to 11 August 1840 in the very words GC quotes, hedged ('may be expected to be broken... And this, I believe, will be found to be the case'); the same article added a further hedge GC omits ('no positive evidence that the first period was exactly, to a day, fulfilled'). Neither critical nor apologetic literature disputes the quotation's authenticity; the dispute is entirely about fulfillment. The documented diplomatic sequence of the Second Eastern Crisis: the Porte accepted great-power intervention against Muhammad Ali after the collective note of July 1839; the Convention for the Pacification of the Levant was signed at London on 15 July 1840 by the four powers together with the Ottoman plenipotentiary (this is the act GC's phrase 'through her ambassadors accepted the protection of the allied powers' actually describes); the envoy Rifat Bey left Constantinople 5 Aug, his steamer reached Alexandria on 11 Aug and sat in quarantine until the 16th; his first audience with the Pasha was 16 Aug and the written communication of the ultimatum followed on 19 Aug (Wellcome 1874 reproducing the Millerite dossier itself; U.S. FRUS 1879 doc. 518; standard accounts of the Oriental Crisis). The empire was not overthrown: the intervention was undertaken by the Quadruple Alliance AND the Ottoman Empire and preserved it (Ozavci, Dangerous Gifts, OUP 2021, ch. 'Return of the Ashes'); the empire endured until 1922. The academic case study of the Millerite use of this episode is Eric Anderson, 'The Millerite Use of Prophecy: A Case Study of a Striking Fulfillment', in Numbers & Butler (eds.), The Disappointed (Indiana UP, 1987): nothing decisive happened on 11 Aug itself; the 'fulfillment' was constructed retrospectively from newspaper reports in the autumn of 1840; Litch himself noted how few were convinced in New England. Litch abandoned the year-day principle in 1867 and in A Complete Harmony of Daniel and the Apocalypse (1873) no longer defended the 11 Aug fulfillment; Adventist reviewers W. A. Spicer (letter, 30 Nov 1914: the ultimatum 'was not delivered to the Pasha of Egypt on Aug. 11, 1840') and W. W. Prescott (1916: the date 'would not stand the test of historical facts') conceded the mismatch internally. The standing published defense (Uriah Smith; Froom, Prophetic Faith of Our Fathers IV; Edwin de Kock) relocates the fulfillment to Rifat Bey's arrival at Alexandria on 11 Aug, arguing control of war and peace passed out of the Sultan's hands that day. (2) The independence pool. That advent expectation appeared in many lands in the early 19th century is uncontested (Sandeen, The Roots of Fundamentalism, U Chicago Press 1970, treats the British and American revivals as one transatlantic phenomenon; the Phase-1 verifier already ruled the simultaneity claim GC-20-015 supported). What the historiography does not support is isolation and sola-Scriptura derivation: Lacunza's manuscript was translated by Edward Irving in 1827 and, in GC's own words, 'served to deepen the interest already awakening in England' (GC ch. 20, par. 20); Joseph Wolff, GC's leading exemplar, attended the first Albury prophetic conference of 1826 convened by Henry Drummond alongside Irving (documented even by Froom, PFOF vol. 3, an admission against interest); Bengel's views spread through his published writings ('spread throughout Christendom', GC par. 23); Gaussen was set on Daniel by Rollin's Ancient History (GC par. 24); the whole wave worked inside the inherited Protestant historicist year-day tradition (Mede-Newton-Faber lineage) transmitted in print. The mutual independence of Bengel (d. 1752), Lacunza (wrote c. 1790), and Miller (reached his scheme 1816-1818, and historians treat Millerism as largely indigenous) is real and carries the weakened version; genuinely 'isolated' bodies deriving the belief 'solely' from Scripture are not documented, and the best candidate sometimes offered (the Swedish child criers of 1841-43) was an ecstatic trance phenomenon inside the Lutheran pietist revival, not a body arriving at belief by study.
narrative_ru: Досье проверяет два сюжета миллеритской истории: тезис, что адвентистская весть возникла в разных странах независимо, «исключительно через изучение Писания», и рассказ о предсказании Джосайи Литча про падение османской власти 11 августа 1840 года. Цитату Литча книга передаёт точно, с его собственными оговорками и с верной датировкой, поэтому это утверждение переведено в подтверждённые. Сам «исполнившийся точный срок» проверки не выдерживает: Порта приняла защиту держав конвенцией 15 июля 1840 года, ультиматум довели до Мухаммеда Али 16-19 августа, а 11 августа в Александрию лишь прибыл пароход с посланником, и позднее сам Литч отказался от этого толкования. Тезис о возникновении веры «исключительно из Писания» в изолированных группах противоречит задокументированным связям: перевод Лакунсы Ирвингом, конференции в Олбери с участием Вольфа, общая традиция толкования пророчеств; в ослабленной форме («сходные выводы при малом прямом контакте», как у Бенгеля, Лакунсы и Миллера) картина правдоподобна. Оценки сдвинули бы османский документ о формальном акте подчинения именно 11 августа или архивные свидетельства о группах, пришедших к датированному адвентистскому ожиданию вне этих сетей и литературы.

quantifier_audit: solely by the study of the Scriptures

components.component: Bodies of Christians in different lands arrived at the belief that the advent was near, in the early 19th century
components.rationale: Uncontested across the historiography: Sandeen documents a transatlantic millenarian revival c. 1800-1845; GC's own chapter catalogs Wolff, Lacunza, Bengel's school, Gaussen, the Scandinavian criers; the Phase-1 verifier independently ruled the parallel simultaneity claim (GC-20-015) supported. See evidence_review, pool 2.

components.component: These bodies were 'isolated', i.e. mutually unconnected, arriving at the belief independently of one another
components.rationale: The documented cases were networked, several of them by GC's own account: Irving's 1827 translation of Lacunza deepened 'the interest already awakening in England' (GC ch. 20 par. 20); Wolff attended the 1826 Albury conference at the center of the British network (Froom PFOF 3 concedes this); Bengel's influence spread via his publications; the Morning Watch and missionary/print networks tied the wave together. Real mutual independence exists for Bengel, Lacunza, and Miller as individuals separated in time, but no historian documents 'isolated bodies' plural in different lands, and under the burden rule the existential-sounding sentence, functioning rhetorically as a universal independence claim, required exactly that documentation.

components.component: They arrived at this belief 'solely by the study of the Scriptures', excluding transmitted interpretive tradition, publications, and preaching
components.rationale: Every documented case worked inside the inherited Protestant historicist year-day tradition transmitted in print (Mede-Newton-Faber lineage); Gaussen was set on Daniel by Rollin's secular history per GC's own narrative; Miller used the standard historicist apparatus; the Swedish child criers, the best candidate for an isolated body, arrived at their proclamation through ecstatic trance inside a pietist revival, not through study. 'Solely' is an exclusive-source assertion the record contradicts for each identifiable case.

rulings.weakened_version: In several lands, expositors and groups of Christians with little or no direct contact with one another arrived, chiefly through their own study of biblical prophecy, at the belief that the advent was near.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest defense: LeRoy Edwin Froom, The Prophetic Faith of Our Fathers vols. 3-4, which catalogs hundreds of advent expositors on several continents and argues a providential, essentially simultaneous awakening; the SDA encyclopedic tradition follows him. Its real strength is the genuine mutual independence of Bengel (d. 1752), Lacunza (c. 1790), and Miller (1816-1818), who demonstrably did not copy one another. The defense fails for the claim as written because Froom's own catalog documents the connective tissue the sentence denies: Irving translating Lacunza in 1827, Wolff sitting at Albury in 1826, Bengel's printed legacy, the Morning Watch circulation, and the shared historicist method that every party inherited from published commentary. Convergence within one transmitted exegetical culture is what the evidence shows; isolation with Scripture as the sole input is not documented in any case. The confessional location of the defense is noted, not held against it. Phase-1: no defender object exists (disputed skipped the stage); the verifier's diffusion rationale is confirmed and extended here.

sources.ref: E. R. Sandeen, The Roots of Fundamentalism: British and American Millenarianism 1800-1930 (U Chicago Press, 1970), ch. 1 'The Revival of British Millenarism'
sources.independence_note: University-press monograph, the standard treatment of the transatlantic revival; independent of all confessional literature on both sides.

sources.ref: GC ch. 20, pars. 20-24 (source/chapters/gc_ch20.txt): Lacunza translated in London c. 1825, interest 'already awakening in England'; Bengel's writings 'spread throughout Christendom'; Gaussen led to Daniel by Rollin
sources.independence_note: The adjudicated book's own narrative; genealogically independent of Sandeen and evidentially decisive as admission against the 'isolated/solely' framing.

sources.ref: L. E. Froom, The Prophetic Faith of Our Fathers vol. 3 (Review & Herald, 1946), section on the first Albury conference listing Joseph Wolff among the twenty participants (via egwwritings.org)
sources.independence_note: Adventist confessional historiography; used as admission against interest on Wolff's network membership; corroborated by DNB entry on Henry Drummond and standard Albury accounts.

sources.ref: 'Two Hundred Years from Lacunza', JATS (Andrews University) on Irving's 1827 translation and its transmission
sources.independence_note: Adventist scholarly venue conceding the Lacunza-Irving transmission chain; same direction as Sandeen but confessionally located on the claim's own side.

rulings.would_change_this: A peer-reviewed case study documenting a body of Christians in the 1820s-1840s that reached a dated near-advent expectation demonstrably without access to historicist prophetic literature or to the Anglo-American and continental revival networks would move the as-written ruling toward genuinely-open; several such cases would overturn it.
rulings.notes: Load-bearing claim. Rowe, Doan, and Numbers & Butler were not directly consulted (no OA copies; Land's local AUSS survey is an unreadable scan in this environment); the ruling rests on Sandeen plus transmission facts attested by the book itself and by the claim's own defenders, which deeper consultation is unlikely to reverse. Kept confidence at medium accordingly.

components.component: Litch published, shortly before 11 Aug 1840, the quoted calculation (150 years exactly fulfilled at Deacozes' permission-accession, plus 391 years 15 days, ending 11 Aug 1840) in these words
components.rationale: The wording matches Litch's article in Signs of the Times, and Expositor of Prophecy of 1 Aug 1840 (GC 1911 Appendix note 560), reproduced identically in apologetic and critical literature alike; no published party disputes authenticity. The idiosyncratic 'Deacozes' and the 391y15d scheme are Litch's own, documented from his 1838 book in the insider record (Wellcome 1874, local file, lines 3841-3862, 6996-7069). GC preserves his hedges ('may be expected', 'And this, I believe, will be found to be the case') and correctly sequences the loose 1838 form ('sometime in the month of August') against the day-precise 1 Aug 1840 refinement ('only a few days previous... he wrote'). See evidence_review, pool 1.

components.component: The prophetic calculation itself: Rev 9 trumpets as Ottoman history under the year-day principle
components.rationale: Out of scope under charter section 9: the adjudication rules on the accuracy of the quotation act, not on whether the year-day computation is valid. The claim-level ruling is conditional in this respect: it certifies attribution, not the soundness of what is attributed.

rulings.weakened_version: In the Signs of the Times of 1 August 1840 Litch published a calculation ending the Ottoman prophetic periods on 11 August 1840, himself conditioning the result on the exactness of the starting dates.
rulings.minority_engagement: No published minority denies the attribution; critics of the episode (Anderson in The Disappointed; Casebolt; counter-apologetic sites) treat the quotation as genuine and attack the fulfillment instead. Answer to the Phase-1 verifier, whose 'disputed' rested on the day-precision being 'refined afterward' and on Litch's later retreat: both points are real but neither touches this claim, since GC's own text states the refinement sequence accurately and Litch's later abandonment (year-day dropped 1867; A Complete Harmony, 1873) bears on GC-18-120's fulfillment framing, not on whether he wrote these words in 1840. One caveat recorded: the same 1 Aug 1840 article contained a further hedge GC omits ('no positive evidence that the first period was exactly, to a day, fulfilled'), but the excerpt GC prints keeps the conditional 'Allowing...' and 'may be expected', so the omission does not alter the quoted meaning. No defender object exists (disputed skipped the stage).

sources.ref: I. C. Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874), local file lines 3841-3862 and 6996-7069: the 1838 calculation, the 391y15d scheme, the Deacozes narrative
sources.independence_note: Insider movement history reproducing Litch's own text; predates GC 1888/1911 and is independent of White's compilation.

sources.ref: E. Anderson, 'The Millerite Use of Prophecy', in Numbers & Butler (eds.), The Disappointed (Indiana UP, 1987)
sources.independence_note: Peer-located academic case study of this exact episode; consulted via convergent secondary reports (Casebolt/Spectrum, essaysbyellenwhite, nonegw), which descend from it and are counted as one conduit; treats the quotation as authentic.

sources.ref: nonegw.org, 'Josiah Litch's Fake Date' (fetched): quotes the 1 Aug 1840 article verbatim and Litch's 1873 Complete Harmony
sources.independence_note: Counter-apologetic site; hostile to the fulfillment claim yet confirming the quotation's authenticity, i.e. adversarial corroboration.

sources.ref: GC 1911 Appendix note 560 (Signs of the Times, and Expositor of Prophecy, 1 Aug 1840)
sources.independence_note: The 1911 edition's own source apparatus, produced in the quote-verification revision; identifies the printed source critics have checked against.

rulings.would_change_this: Examination of the physical/scanned Signs of the Times, and Expositor of Prophecy issue of 1 August 1840 showing different wording, or absence of the passage, would overturn the attribution; a documented pre-1840 source for the exact 11 Aug wording would also revise the 'a few days previous' framing.
rulings.notes: Ruling moves toward the book (disputed -> well-supported), per charter section 7 symmetry. The Phase-1 'disputed' conflated attribution accuracy with fulfillment validity.

components.component: Turkey, through her ambassadors, accepted the protection/intervention of the allied powers of Europe in 1840
components.rationale: Documented: the Porte accepted great-power intervention against Muhammad Ali (collective note of July 1839) and its plenipotentiary signed the Convention for the Pacification of the Levant at London, 15 July 1840, with the four powers. See evidence_review, pool 1.

components.component: This acceptance occurred 'at the very time specified' by Litch's calculation, i.e. 11 August 1840
components.rationale: The dated record contradicts the day-match: the acceptance 'through her ambassadors' happened at London on 15 July 1840; the ultimatum reached Muhammad Ali on 16-19 August (Rifat Bey's first audience 16 Aug, written communication 19 Aug per FRUS 1879 doc. 518); the only 11 Aug occurrence was the arrival of Rifat Bey's steamer at Alexandria, where it sat in quarantine until the 16th, a detail the Millerite dossier itself records (Wellcome 1874, lines 7309-7316). Adventist investigators Spicer (1914) and Prescott (1916) conceded the mismatch. Capped at improbable rather than discredited because GC par. 46 prints both the loose 1838 form ('sometime in the month of August') and the day-precise form, and under the month-loose reading the mid-August ultimatum events do fall within the specified period.

components.component: 'and thus placed herself under the control of Christian nations'
components.rationale: The intervention was undertaken by the Quadruple Alliance and the Ottoman Empire jointly and preserved the empire (Ozavci 2021); the empire remained sovereign until 1922, entering the Tanzimat era, with growing but informal European leverage. Contemporary press rhetoric (London Morning Herald: the Sultan 'reduced to the rank of a puppet'; Goodell in Missionary Herald 1841) gives the frame period currency, but modern Ottoman historiography does not describe an August-1840 passage under Christian 'control'.

rulings.weakened_version: In the summer of 1840, within weeks of the date Litch had named, the Ottoman government accepted four-power intervention against Muhammad Ali (Convention of London, 15 July 1840; ultimatum communicated to the Pasha in mid-August), and contemporaries widely read the crisis as exposing the empire's dependence on Christian Europe.
rulings.minority_engagement: Strongest defense: the line running from Litch's own November 1840 letter through Uriah Smith, Froom (PFOF IV), and Edwin de Kock ('Vindicating Josiah Litch's Dates'): Rifat Bey arrived at Alexandria on 11 August, and from that moment 'the question of war or peace was taken from the Sultan's hands', so Ottoman supremacy departed on the calculated day. Engaged on evidence, it fails for the sentence as written: (a) a steamer's arrival in quarantine effected no transaction, and even the Millerite dossier concedes the envoy could not act until the 16th; (b) the act GC actually describes, acceptance 'through her ambassadors', is the London Convention of 15 July; (c) Litch's quoted prediction concerned Ottoman power 'in Constantinople', where nothing occurred on 11 August; (d) Litch himself dropped the year-day basis in 1867 and no longer defended the fulfillment in 1873, and Adventist reviewers Spicer and Prescott privately conceded the date. The defense keeps a real insight, that contemporaries saw 1840 as the end of Ottoman self-sufficiency, which the weakened ruling credits in full. No Phase-1 defender object exists (disputed skipped the stage); the verifier's rationale is confirmed with tightened dating.

sources.ref: O. Ozavci, Dangerous Gifts: Imperialism, Security, and Civil Wars in the Levant, 1798-1864 (Oxford UP, 2021), ch. 'Return of the Ashes: The Concert of Europe and the 1840 Intervention'
sources.independence_note: Current university-press treatment of the 1840 intervention; independent of all Millerite-related literature.

sources.ref: U.S. Foreign Relations of the United States 1879, doc. 518 (history.state.gov): written communication of the offer/ultimatum to Muhammad Ali dated 19 August 1840
sources.independence_note: Diplomatic compilation genealogically independent of both Adventist and counter-Adventist positions.

sources.ref: I. C. Wellcome, History of the Second Advent Message (1874), local file lines 7200-7353: Moniteur Ottoman 22 Aug 1840, Convention of 15 July, Rifat Bey's 5 Aug departure, 11 Aug arrival, quarantine to the 16th
sources.independence_note: The Millerite movement's own dossier; its dates are admissions from the claim's defenders and independent of modern scholarship.

sources.ref: E. Anderson, 'The Millerite Use of Prophecy', in The Disappointed (Indiana UP, 1987), with W. A. Spicer letter 30 Nov 1914 and W. W. Prescott 1916 as reported there and in derivative literature
sources.independence_note: Academic case study consulted via convergent secondary reports (Spectrum/Casebolt, essaysbyellenwhite, nonegw), counted as one genealogical line.

sources.ref: Convention of London (1840): four powers plus Ottoman plenipotentiary, 15 July 1840 (textbook fact; encyclopedic corroboration)
sources.independence_note: Date and parties uncontested across all sources; corroborates, does not carry, the ruling.

rulings.would_change_this: An Ottoman or European archival instrument dated 11 August 1840 recording a formal act of Ottoman acceptance or submission executed that day (not a courier's arrival) would rehabilitate the date component; a body of current Ottomanist scholarship dating the empire's effective loss of sovereignty to 1840 would rehabilitate the frame.
rulings.notes: Tie at the claim minimum between the date component (fact) and the control frame; failing_component_kind takes fact by the section 3 priority, tied_kinds records both.
